days.title: Waking up: събуждам се

vocab.exampleEn: I wake up at seven o'clock.
I get up slowly in the morning.
In the morning I drink coffee.
The alarm clock rings early.
He gets up very early.
On Sunday I get up late.
The bed is by the window.

grammar.title: Reflexive verbs with се (introduction)
grammar.explanation: Many Bulgarian daily-routine verbs use the little word 'се' (oneself). With these verbs the action turns back on the doer: 'събуждам' means 'I wake (someone)', but 'събуждам се' means 'I wake up (myself)'. The 'се' does not change form for person — it stays 'се' for I, you, he, we, they. In a simple statement it usually comes right after the verb: 'Събуждам се рано' (I wake up early). Compare with a non-reflexive verb like 'ставам' (I get up), which needs no 'се'. Note Bulgarian has a real present-tense verb 'to be': съм, си, е, сме, сте, са — used like English 'am/is/are'.

examples.english: I wake up at six o'clock.
You wake up early.

examples.russian: Аз съм буден, но ставам бавно.
examples.english: I am awake, but I get up slowly.

reading.title: Сутрин
reading.text: Будилникът звъни рано. Събуждам се, но ставам бавно. Сутрин пия кафе.
reading.translation: The alarm clock rings early. I wake up, but I get up slowly. In the morning I drink coffee.

days.speakingPrompt: Describe when you wake up and get up in the morning. Use 'събуждам се' and 'ставам' with a time (в седем часа) and the words 'рано' or 'късно'.

days.title: Washing up: измивам се

vocab.example: Сутрин се измивам със студена вода.
vocab.exampleEn: In the morning I wash with cold water.
I wash my teeth every morning.
My face is clean.
The water is cold.
The bathroom is small.
The soap smells nice.
The towel is by the bathroom.

grammar.title: Where 'се' goes in a sentence
grammar.explanation: The reflexive 'се' is a short clitic word — it cannot stand at the very start of a sentence. Normally it sits right after the verb: 'Измивам се' (I wash up). But if another word comes first (like 'сутрин', 'после', or a subject pronoun), 'се' jumps to BEFORE the verb: 'Сутрин се измивам' (In the morning I wash up); 'Аз се измивам' (I wash myself). Both word orders are correct; only the position of 'се' shifts. Remember: 'мия' = wash something (мия зъбите = wash the teeth), while 'измивам се' = wash oneself.

examples.english: I wash with cold water.
In the morning I wash up in the bathroom.
Afterwards I wash my face.

reading.title: В банята
reading.text: Сутрин се измивам в банята. Водата е студена. Сапунът мирише хубаво.
reading.translation: In the morning I wash up in the bathroom. The water is cold. The soap smells nice.

days.speakingPrompt: Say what you do in the bathroom in the morning. Use 'измивам се' and 'мия', and put 'сутрин' or 'после' first so you practise moving 'се' before the verb.

days.title: Getting dressed: обличам се

vocab.exampleEn: I get dressed quickly in the morning.

vocab.example: Вечер се събличам и си лягам.
vocab.exampleEn: In the evening I get undressed and go to bed.
The clothes are on the bed.
The shirt is white.
The trousers are new.
The shoes are by the door.
I get dressed very fast.

grammar.title: Reflexive pairs: обличам се / събличам се
grammar.explanation: Reflexive verbs often come in opposite pairs that share the same 'се'. 'Обличам се' = I dress myself; 'събличам се' = I undress myself. The verb part changes endings for person, but 'се' stays the same: обличам се, обличаш се, облича се, обличаме се, обличате се, обличат се. To talk about putting on one specific item without 'се', you can use the object directly: 'обличам ризата' (I put on the shirt). Note: to say you go to bed we use 'лягам си' — here the short word is 'си', a cousin of 'се' that we will meet tomorrow.

examples.english: I get dressed quickly.
In the evening I get undressed.
The children get dressed by themselves.

reading.title: Дрехите
reading.text: Обличам се бързо сутрин. Ризата е бяла, а панталонът е нов. Обувките са до вратата.
reading.translation: I get dressed quickly in the morning. The shirt is white, and the trousers are new. The shoes are by the door.

days.speakingPrompt: Describe how you get dressed in the morning and undressed at night. Use 'обличам се' and 'събличам се' plus two clothing words, and add 'бързо' or 'бавно'.

days.title: Combing and getting ready: реша се, приготвям се

vocab.exampleEn: I comb my hair in front of the mirror.
I get ready for work.
My hair is long.
The mirror is in the bathroom.
The brush is on the table.
I am ready for work.
In the evening I go to bed early.

grammar.title: The short word 'си' vs 'се'
grammar.explanation: Besides 'се', Bulgarian has a second little reflexive word: 'си'. We use 'си' when the action is done for or to oneself, often with one's own things: 'мия си зъбите' (I brush my teeth), 'лягам си' (I lie down / go to bed), 'почивам си' (I rest). 'Се' is the plain reflexive 'oneself' (обличам се). Like 'се', the word 'си' stays the same for every person and follows the same placement rule: after the verb, but before it when another word starts the sentence — 'Вечер си лягам рано'. Use 'съм' for 'I am ready': 'Готов съм'.

examples.english: I comb my hair in front of the mirror.
In the evening I go to bed early.
Now I am ready.

reading.title: Готов съм
reading.text: Реша се пред огледалото. После се приготвям за работа. Сега съм готов.
reading.translation: I comb my hair in front of the mirror. Then I get ready for work. Now I am ready.

days.speakingPrompt: Describe the last steps of getting ready and going to bed. Use 'реша се', 'приготвям се' and 'лягам си', and finish with 'Готов съм' or 'Готова съм'.

days.title: How I feel: чувствам се

vocab.exampleEn: I feel well today.
Today I am fine.
In the evening I am tired.
Today I am a little ill.
I feel happy.
Now I feel better.
I work all day.

grammar.title: Saying how you feel with 'чувствам се'
grammar.explanation: 'Чувствам се' (I feel) is followed by an ADVERB, not an adjective, when you describe a state: 'Чувствам се добре' (I feel well), 'Чувствам се зле' (I feel bad). To describe yourself with an adjective, you usually drop 'чувствам се' and use the verb 'to be' (съм): 'Уморен съм' (I am tired), 'Болен съм' (I am ill). The adjective then agrees with you: a man says 'уморен съм', a woman 'уморена съм'. Remember 'се' shifts before the verb after another word: 'Сега се чувствам по-добре'.

examples.english: I feel well today.
In the evening I am tired (woman speaking).
Now I feel better.

reading.title: Как се чувствам
reading.text: Работя цял ден и вечер съм уморен. Но сега се чувствам добре. Утре ще съм щастлив.
reading.translation: I work all day and in the evening I am tired. But now I feel well. Tomorrow I will be happy.

days.speakingPrompt: Say how you feel at different times of day. Use 'чувствам се' with an adverb (добре, зле) and 'съм' with an adjective (уморен/уморена, щастлив/щастлива).

days.title: My whole day: the routine together

vocab.exampleEn: I come back home in the evening.
I usually get up at seven.
I have breakfast, then I leave.
I have breakfast in the kitchen.
I leave for work at eight.
In the evening I rest.
I do this every day.

grammar.title: Linking the routine: putting reflexive verbs in sequence
grammar.explanation: Now we connect the whole day with sequence words: 'обикновено' (usually), 'после' (then), 'после' again, 'вечер' (in the evening). Each step can mix plain verbs and reflexive ones, and the placement rule for 'се'/'си' still applies. When a sentence starts with 'обикновено', 'после' or 'вечер', the short word moves before the verb: 'После се обличам', 'Вечер се връщам вкъщи'. Use 'и' (and) or 'после' to chain steps: 'Събуждам се, измивам се и се обличам.' Notice in a list with one shared 'и' the 'се' attaches to the nearest verb naturally.

examples.english: I usually wake up at seven.
Then I get dressed and have breakfast.

examples.russian: Вечер се връщам вкъщи и си почивам.
examples.english: In the evening I come back home and rest.

reading.title: Моят ден
reading.text: Обикновено се събуждам в седем. После се измивам, обличам се и закусвам. Вечер се връщам вкъщи уморен.
reading.translation: I usually wake up at seven. Then I wash up, get dressed and have breakfast. In the evening I come back home tired.

days.speakingPrompt: Tell the story of your whole day in order. Use at least four reflexive verbs from this week and link them with 'обикновено', 'после' and 'вечер'.

days.title: Review: the reflexive day

grammar.title: Recap: 'се' and 'си' in the daily routine
grammar.explanation: This week you learned reflexive verbs with 'се': събуждам се (wake up), измивам се (wash up), обличам се / събличам се (get dressed/undressed), реша се (comb my hair), приготвям се (get ready), чувствам се (feel), връщам се (return). You also met the cousin word 'си' for actions done to one's own things: лягам си (go to bed), мия си зъбите (brush my teeth), почивам си (rest). Key rules: (1) 'се' and 'си' never change for person; (2) they follow the verb, but move BEFORE it when another word opens the clause (Сутрин се обличам); (3) use 'чувствам се' + adverb (добре) but 'съм' + adjective (уморен/уморена); (4) Bulgarian uses real 'to be' forms съм, си, е, сме, сте, са, has no cases, and shows relations with prepositions (в, на, до, за).

examples.russian: Сутрин се събуждам рано и ставам бавно.
examples.english: In the morning I wake up early and get up slowly.

examples.russian: После се измивам, обличам се и се реша.
examples.english: Then I wash up, get dressed and comb my hair.

examples.russian: Вечер съм уморен, но се чувствам добре.
examples.english: In the evening I am tired, but I feel well.

reading.title: Диалог: Как мина денят?
reading.text: — Как се чувстваш сега? — Уморен съм, защото работих цял ден. — А сутрин? — Сутрин се събудих рано, измих се и се облякох бързо.
reading.translation: — How do you feel now? — I am tired, because I worked all day. — And in the morning? — In the morning I woke up early, washed up and got dressed quickly.

days.speakingPrompt: Have a short dialogue about your day: ask 'Как се чувстваш?' and answer, then describe your morning routine using three reflexive verbs with 'се'. Try to move 'се' correctly after 'сутрин' or 'после'.
